items.original: Меня опять терзают смутные сомнения… У Шпака — магнитофон, у посла — медальон
items.translation:
Vague doubts haunt me once again...
Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.reference: Vague doubts haunt me once again... Shpak's tape recorder, the ambassador's medallion.
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation and preserves the mock-heroic register of «терзают смутные сомнения». It also correctly avoids a possessive reading (“has”) and instead frames it as an associative clue list, matching the intended joke/callback.

items.original: Здравствуйте, царь, очень приятно. Царь, очень приятно, царь. Очень приятно, здравствуйте, царь.
items.translation: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.reference: Hello, I’m the Tsar, very pleased to meet you. The Tsar, very pleased. Tsar, very pleased. Hello, the Tsar.
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation. The repetitive, banal politeness is preserved, keeping the era-clash humor (Soviet social cliché in a royal setting) and Bunsha’s drunken awkwardness.

items.translation: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.reference: I demand that the banquet continue!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation. Keeps the sudden authoritarian, pompous demand that is central to the joke (uptight bureaucrat adopting royal power-hunger).

items.original: Оставь меня, старушка, я в печали
items.translation: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.reference: Leave me, old woman, I am in sorrow.
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation. Preserves the elevated/archaic melancholy and the comic mismatch with the mundane Soviet domestic situation; “old woman” keeps the intended insult/misaddress.

items.translation: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.reference: They've walled me in... Walled me in, the demons!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation. Correctly treats “demons” as the agent and keeps the paranoid-mystical framing of a mundane technical malfunction, which is the comedic core.

items.translation: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.reference: Behold the power of the life-giving cross!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation. Maintains the mock-biblical proclamation and the (for Soviet context) pointed religious irony that the Tsar attributes a mechanical event to faith.

items.translation: The floor is open to everyone!
items.reference: The floor is open to everyone!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation. Captures the emcee-like command and the mass-party vibe; works as a culturally legible equivalent rather than a flat literal “Everyone dance!”.

items.translation: Now, this was a good call!
items.reference: Now, this was a good call!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation. Preserves the breezy self-satisfaction and the comic understatement of a burglar delighted by his “lucky” timing.

items.original: Икра чёрная, красная… Да! Заморская икра… баклажанная!
items.translation: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.reference: Black caviar, red caviar... and yes! Overseas spread... eggplant!
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation. Crucially avoids calling the eggplant item “caviar” in English, preserving the Soviet-satire inversion without confusing viewers, while keeping the comic pause/beat before the reveal.

items.original: Три магнитофона, три кинокамеры, три портсигара отечественных, куртка… замшевая… Три
items.translation: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.reference: Three tape recorders, three movie cameras, three cigarette cases, domestic... a suede jacket... Three.
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation. Keeps the escalating inventory rhythm and the comic self-incrimination/boastfulness, including the trailing “Three” that signals exaggeration and muddled counting.

items.original: И тебя вылечат… и тебя тоже вылечат… и меня вылечат…
items.translation: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.reference: And they’ll cure you... and they’ll cure you too... and they’ll cure me...
items.analysis: Identical to the approved translation. Preserves the calm, sing-song fatalism that turns a frightening reality (psychiatric commitment) into shared absurdist comedy.

summary.interpretation: 0.00 MQM penalty points per meaning unit (≈ 0.00 major errors per unit; weights: minor=1, major=5, critical=10)
